verses.text: The Preacher opens with his theme: 'Vanity of vanities... all is vanity,' asking what profit man gains from all his toil under the sun. Earthly life, viewed apart from God, is empty. Nothing on earth alone can satisfy or last.

The Preacher opens with his theme: 'Vanity of vanities... all is vanity,' asking what profit man gains from all his toil under the sun. Earthly life, viewed apart from God, is empty. Nothing on earth alone can satisfy or last.

The Preacher opens with his theme: 'Vanity of vanities... all is vanity,' asking what profit man gains from all his toil under the sun. Earthly life, viewed apart from God, is empty. Nothing on earth alone can satisfy or last.

Generations pass while the earth abides; the sun, wind, and rivers circle endlessly yet reach no rest. Nature labours on without arriving. The ceaseless round of the world mirrors the restlessness of a life lived only here below.

All things weary, the eye is never satisfied, 'there is no new thing under the sun,' and former things are forgotten. Novelty and fame both fade. Whatever men chase soon palls, and even memory of them dies.

Solomon gave his heart to search out all things by wisdom and found it 'sore travail,' for 'that which is crooked cannot be made straight.' Even the wisest cannot mend the world. Human effort meets limits it can never cross.

Solomon gave his heart to search out all things by wisdom and found it 'sore travail,' for 'that which is crooked cannot be made straight.' Even the wisest cannot mend the world. Human effort meets limits it can never cross.

Though he gained great wisdom, he learned that 'in much wisdom is much grief: and he that increaseth knowledge increaseth sorrow.' Learning alone does not content. The more a man understands of life's emptiness, the heavier his heart grows.
